mappings.rationaleAI: Lunar exploration by spacecraft (Luna, Apollo, Artemis programs) directly addresses the use of unmanned and manned spacecraft to probe the Moon (C.2.b.iii). The Moon's role in advancing planetary science connects to contributions of space exploration to the physical sciences (C.4).

Spaceflight comprehensively covers the history of space flight (C.1), launch vehicles (C.2.a), crewed programs including Soyuz, Apollo, and Space Shuttle (C.2.c), suborbital and orbital trajectory types (C.3.d.i), reentry thermal management (C.3.d.iv), and life support challenges (D). The treatment of space medicine and weightlessness connects to life-support systems for exploration.

Rocket propulsion systems generating thrust via reaction to high-speed exhaust are the primary space launch vehicles (C.2.a). Multistage designs, specific impulse, and staging of propulsion systems directly address launch principles and techniques (C.3.c).

Covers the full history of space flight from Sputnik through Artemis (C.1, C.2), robotic planetary missions (C.2.b), crewed programs (C.2.c), and the broader significance of exploration for science (C.4). The Cold War Space Race and commercial launch vehicle development provide historical context for space programs since 1957.

Radar as a geophysical method using radio waves for remote detection applies to indirect methods of surface exploration (A.2.a). Acoustic and radar methods for oceanographic measurement connect to developments in oceanographic sampling and measurement techniques (B.3).

Orbiting satellites for scientific purposes such as Earth observation and communications (C.2.b.ii) and Earth-oriented satellites for communications, survey, and navigation (C.2.d) are directly covered. Spacecraft subsystems including solar panels and transponders relate to technology of spacecraft subsystems (C.3.b).

Oceans and seas as the domain of undersea exploration broadly connect to techniques and equipment of undersea exploration (B), including oceanographic science, seawater chemistry, and deep-water habitats that exploration technology targets.
Navigation methods including GNSS, inertial navigation, and radar apply to navigational methods for establishing precise location of undersea discoveries (B.2) and navigation in space (C.3.d.ii).

Mars as a target continuously explored by probes since 1997 and a primary target for future crewed missions directly addresses unmanned spacecraft probing the planets (C.2.b.iii). Geological discoveries on Mars contribute to advances in the physical sciences through space exploration (C.4).
Natural satellites of planets — from Io's volcanism to Europa's subsurface ocean — are studied through unmanned spacecraft probing planetary satellites (C.2.b.iii).

Geology underpins surface and underground exploration, including scientific determination of the Earth's interior properties (A.1.a), resource exploration for ores, fuels, and water (A.1.b), and engineering geology for construction planning (A.1.c).

Laser applications including lidar relate to developments in oceanographic measurement techniques (B.3) and to spacecraft subsystem technology where lasers serve communication and ranging functions (C.3.b).

The Soviet space programme including Sputnik, Gagarin's orbital flight, and the Vostok/Voskhod/Soyuz programs are central to the history of space flight prior to and after Sputnik I (C.1) and manned space programs of the U.S.S.R. (C.2.c).

Antarctic exploration as an extreme environment for scientific determination of Earth's properties (A.1.a) and the life-support challenges of exploration in polar conditions (D) are directly relevant.
Civil engineering's geotechnical sub-discipline directly addresses exploration for construction — planning of tunnels, foundations, and other works (A.1.c).

The Pacific Ocean as Earth's largest oceanic division, including Mariana Trench exploration, directly connects to undersea exploration platforms (B.1) and oceanographic sampling and measurement techniques (B.3).
Measurement as the quantification of physical attributes underpins geophysical methods of exploration (A.2.a) and oceanographic measurement techniques (B.3).
AI systems for autonomous control and decision-making in spacecraft connect to technology of spacecraft subsystems (C.3.b), where autonomous and telerobotic control are key operational capabilities.
